transcript:
You're watching the same word say itself over and over — but look at how it says it. It's not fading in and out smoothly. Watch closely and you'll catch a flicker, a stutter in the middle of each appearance before it cuts to black. That's deliberate: in the draw loop there's a comment that says "not sine — sine makes every cycle identical. This piece is about how they're not." So instead of a smooth pulse, the word attacks fast, holds with a jittery flicker, then cuts hard to dark.

And here's the part that gets me: each time the word finishes speaking, it leaves a ring behind — literally called "leave trace" in the code — and those rings pile up in an array called prior readings. As that history grows, the next cycle takes longer to complete, because the code literally adds context weight, more rings, more delay before "again" surfaces again.

So the identical instruction — draw the same word, same spot, same size — is quietly bending under the weight of its own past.

cues.target: Not sin()—sin makes every cycle identical. This piece is about how they're not.
